title: Illegal foreign vessel, fishers seized by ABF in Cape York river, eyewitnesses say
author: Brendan Mounter, Adam Stephen, and Christopher Testa
fullText: An illegal foreign fishing vessel and nine men have been intercepted by Australian Border Force (ABF) off the far northern coast of Queensland, according to eyewitness reports.Rusty Tully, who runs a pearl farm on Turtle Head Island about 30 kilometres east of Bamaga, said he saw the vessel being towed by the ABF about 9am this morning.Mr Tully said he had alerted the ABF on Monday evening after he and his wife, Bronwyn Tully, spotted a group of men walking in the remote area through mangroves near Escape River, more than 1,000km north of Cairns."The [ABF officers] came in and I showed them on Google Earth where I suspected they (the men) might be," he said."They've headed over there and 30 minutes later, they've pulled out this big, horrible wooden boat that you wouldn't be able to sell in Australia."It was quite professional … they've got a job to do and they did it well."Rusty Tully says he reported the illegal foreign fishing vessel to the ABF last night. (Supplied: Rusty Tully)The ABF would not confirm the ABC's enquiries about the interception including how many fishers were detained or the contents of the boat.A spokesperson said the ABF was undertaking operational activity as part of Operation Broadstaff and it was inappropriate to comment further on an ongoing operation.Boat could be destroyedThe federal assistant minister responsible for the agency, Julian Hill, said foreign fishers entering Australian waters illegally risk losing their boats.Mr Hill made a two-day trip to the region last week as part of an ongoing response to an influx of illegal foreign fishing boats."We've already destroyed six out of the 15 boats intercepted [since January] and we leave it to the Australian Border Force, case-by-case, to determine the best response," he said."If you destroy a boat, you've then got to deal with the crew and the equipment."It depends on the type of boat, it depends on the operating environment. They're judgements for Border Force to make."Sunken foreign boats to be salvagedMeanwhile, the Australian Fisheries Management Authority (AFMA) said two illegal foreign fishing vessels found sunken in far north Queensland waters should be salvaged within the next fortnight.AFMA said along with Parks Australia, it would undertake the recovery of a boat found off Masig in the Torres Strait and another near Crab Island, in the Northern Peninsula Area.An abandoned illegal foreign fishing vessel before and after it washed up on Crab Island off the far north Queensland coast in early February.  (Supplied: David Kempton)Masig traditional owner Simon Naawi has cautiously welcomed the news."We don't want to put the cart before the horse, but when we see it retrieved from that reef, we'll be most happy," he said.Mr Naawi believes the boat sank about a fortnight ago and said its removal, along with its large ghost net, was a matter of urgency to protect the marine life his people rely on."Our bread and butter within this vicinity are our surrounding reefs," he said."They are like a garden to us. That vessel sitting there, it needs to be salvaged ASAP."
summary: An illegal foreign fishing vessel and nine men aboard have been intercepted by Australian Border Force off the far northern coast of Queensland, according to eyewitness reports.
